---
title: "Module 10: Troubleshooting and Recovery"
description: "Undo mistakes, escape detached HEAD, recover lost commits, and restore working directory changes."
module: 10
prerequisites:
  - 0
  - 1
  - 2
learning_objectives:
  - "Learner can restore working directory changes with git restore"
  - "Learner can unstage changes and undo commits with git reset"
  - "Learner can use git reflog to find and recover lost commits"
  - "Learner can escape a detached HEAD state safely"
  - "Learner can diagnose repository problems with git fsck"
platform_coverage:
  - macos
  - linux
  - windows-gitbash
  - windows-wsl2
---

## Overview

Every Git user eventually makes a mistake: a commit with wrong content, an accidental
`git reset --hard`, or confusion after detaching HEAD. Git's recovery tools are
surprisingly powerful — almost nothing is truly lost within the first 30 days. This
module covers the full toolkit for undoing, recovering, and diagnosing problems.

## Prerequisites

- [Module 02 — Basic Workflow](02-basic-workflow.qmd)
- [Module 01 — Git Concepts](01-git-concepts.qmd) (for understanding objects and refs)

---

### Reading Git Error Messages

Git messages have four severity levels:

| Prefix | Severity | Meaning |
|--------|----------|---------|
| `fatal:` | Highest | Operation could not start or complete — Git stopped entirely |
| `error:` | High | An operation failed; Git may have partially applied changes |
| `warning:` | Medium | Something unusual happened; operation continued |
| `hint:` | Informational | Git suggestion — often tells you exactly how to fix the problem |

**Strategy**: Always read `hint:` lines first — Git frequently tells you the fix directly.

```text
hint: Updates were rejected because the remote contains work that you do not
hint: have locally. Integrate the remote changes (e.g.
hint: 'git pull --rebase ...') before pushing again.
```

The fix is in the hint: `git pull --rebase`.

---

### Undoing Working Directory Changes: git restore

Discard unstaged changes to a file — restore it to its last committed state:

```bash
git restore scripts/analysis.R
```

Discard all unstaged changes across all files:

```bash
git restore .
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## git restore Is Permanent for Unstaged Changes

Changes discarded with `git restore` **cannot be recovered**. They were never in a
commit or the stash, so Git has no record of them. Double-check that you do not
need the changes before running this command.
:::

**Restore a file to how it looked in a specific commit**:

```bash
git restore --source 3c4d5e6 scripts/analysis.R
```

This does not change the commit history — it just updates your working directory.
The file will appear as "modified" in `git status`.

---

### Undoing Staged Changes: git restore --staged

Move a staged file back to the working directory (unstage it):

```bash
git restore --staged scripts/analysis.R
```

```text
(no output — the file is back in working directory, unstaged)
```

Verify:

```bash
git status
```

```text
On branch main
Changes not staged for commit:
  (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)
  (use "git restore <file>..." to discard changes in working directory)
        modified:   scripts/analysis.R
```

The change is still in your working directory — you just removed it from the staging
area. The file is not lost.

---

### Undoing Commits: git reset

`git reset` moves the current branch pointer backwards. Three modes control what
happens to the changes from the undone commits:

| Mode | Branch pointer | Staging area | Working directory |
|------|---------------|--------------|-------------------|
| `--soft` | Moved back | Keeps changes staged | Unchanged |
| `--mixed` (default) | Moved back | Unstages changes | Unchanged |
| `--hard` | Moved back | Cleared | **Changes discarded** |

**--soft: Undo commit, keep changes staged**

```bash
git reset HEAD~1 --soft
```

The last commit is undone. All its changes are staged, ready to recommit.
Use this to fix a commit message or split a commit.

**--mixed: Undo commit, keep changes in working directory**

```bash
git reset HEAD~1
```

(Default mode — `--mixed` is implied.) The commit is undone. Changes are in your
working directory but not staged. Use this to reorganize which files go into a commit.

**--hard: Undo commit, discard all changes**

```bash
git reset HEAD~1 --hard
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## --hard Discards Work Permanently (Usually)

`git reset --hard` discards the changes from the undone commits. They are not staged,
not in the working directory, and not in any commit.

**However**: If the discarded commits were already committed (they were reachable from
some branch), they are still in `.git/objects/` and retrievable via `git reflog`
for approximately 30 days until garbage collection.

Use `--hard` only when you are certain you want to discard the changes.
:::

---

### git reflog: Your Safety Net

The reflog records every movement of `HEAD` — every commit, reset, checkout, and branch
switch. It is your recovery tool for almost any mistake.

```bash
git reflog
```

```text
7e8f9a0 (HEAD -> main) HEAD@{0}: reset: moving to HEAD~1
3c4d5e6 HEAD@{1}: commit: Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
9d4c7e1 HEAD@{2}: commit: Add: normalize_balance function
3f2a1b4 HEAD@{3}: commit: Add: initial analysis script
1a5f3b2 HEAD@{4}: checkout: moving from feature/grip to main
```

Each entry shows: `hash (ref) HEAD@{N}: action: description`

`HEAD@{0}` is the current position. `HEAD@{1}` is where HEAD was before the last action.

**Recover a "lost" commit** after `git reset --hard`:

```bash
# Identify the hash of the commit you want to recover
git reflog
# Find the hash — e.g., 3c4d5e6

# Option 1: Check it out to verify the content
git checkout 3c4d5e6
# (you are now in detached HEAD — see below)

# Option 2: Create a branch at that commit immediately
git switch -c recovered-work 3c4d5e6
```

---

### Detached HEAD: Understanding and Fixing

"Detached HEAD" means `HEAD` points directly to a commit hash rather than to a
branch name.

**How it happens**:

```bash
git checkout 3c4d5e6       # checking out a commit hash
git checkout v1.0.0        # checking out a tag
gh pr checkout 47          # checking out a PR
```

**What git status shows**:

```bash
git status
```

```text
HEAD detached at 3c4d5e6
nothing to commit, working tree clean
```

**What it means**: You can look at files and even make commits — but those commits
are not on any branch. As soon as you switch to another branch, they become unreachable
(though still recoverable via reflog for ~30 days).

**Fix 1: Go back to a branch without saving anything**

```bash
git switch main
```

**Fix 2: Save commits made in detached HEAD onto a new branch**

```bash
# (while still in detached HEAD, after making some commits)
git switch -c rescue-branch
```

Now your commits are safely on `rescue-branch` and HEAD is attached.

---

### Recovering Lost Commits

Full recovery scenario:

```bash
# You ran git reset --hard one too many times
git log --oneline
```

```text
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function
3f2a1b4 Add: initial analysis script
```

The "Fix: use z-score normalization" commit is gone from `git log`. Recover it:

```bash
git reflog
```

```text
9d4c7e1 (HEAD -> main) HEAD@{0}: reset: moving to HEAD~1
3c4d5e6 HEAD@{1}: commit: Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
9d4c7e1 HEAD@{2}: commit: Add: normalize_balance function
```

```bash
# The lost commit hash is 3c4d5e6
git switch -c recovery-branch 3c4d5e6
```

```text
Switched to a new branch 'recovery-branch'
```

```bash
git log --oneline
```

```text
3c4d5e6 (HEAD -> recovery-branch) Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function
3f2a1b4 Add: initial analysis script
```

Recovered. Now merge or cherry-pick as needed.

---

### git fsck: Repository Health Check

`git fsck` verifies the integrity of the Git object database:

```bash
git fsck
```

```text
Checking object directories: 100% (256/256), done.
Checking connectivity: done.
```

If there are problems:

```text
dangling commit 7e8f9a0b1c2d3e4f5a6b7c8d9e0f1a2b3c4d5e6f
dangling tree 1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a4b5c6d7e8f9a0b
```

Dangling objects are commits or trees not reachable from any branch or tag — often
created by `git reset` or rebases. They are harmless and will be garbage collected
eventually.

With `--lost-found`:

```bash
git fsck --lost-found
```

Unreachable objects are written to `.git/lost-found/`. You can inspect them with
`git show`.

---

### Resetting to Remote State

When your local branch has diverged from the remote and you want to discard all local
changes:

::: {.callout-important}
## This Discards All Local Commits

The following command permanently discards any local commits that are not on the remote.
Make sure you have pushed or stashed anything you want to keep.
:::

```bash
git fetch origin
git reset --hard origin/main
```

```text
HEAD is now at 7e8f9a0 Fix: update inclusion criteria for Wave 2
```

Your local `main` now exactly matches the remote.

**Nuclear option** — destroy and re-clone:

```bash
cd ..
rm -rf rehab-research
git clone git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git
```

::: {.callout-important}
## rm -rf Destroys Everything Permanently

This deletes the entire repository including `.git/`. All local commits not pushed,
all stashes, all reflog history — everything is gone. Only use this if the repository
is completely corrupted and you have confirmed everything important is on the remote.
:::

---

## Hands-on Exercise

**[Exercises 10 →](../exercises/10-exercises.qmd)**

**Quick check**: After using `git reflog`:

```bash
git reflog | head -5
```

You should see the last 5 HEAD movements with their hashes.

## What's Next

In **[Module 11 — Tool Integration](11-tool-integration.qmd)**, you will connect
Git with RStudio, Jupyter, and shell hooks to fit Git naturally into your research
workflow.
